business_info: Some internal planning documents use the term "legacy county" to refer to rural counties whose school boundaries have not changed since the 1970s; analysts sometimes filter reports by these legacy counties even though there is no dedicated column for this concept in the SAT data.

In facilities and capital planning memos, the phrase "oldest schools" occasionally refers to campuses located in the original town center rather than to the literal year the school opened or its CDS code ordering, which can lead to discrepancies between narrative reports and data pulls.

Academic performance dashboards in some regions use an internally defined "SAT excellence index" that caps each section (Reading, Math, Writing) at the 90th percentile and then averages the three capped values; this is different from raw averages shown in the statewide files.

Some county offices informally refer to a school as "virtual-only" if at least 80% of its daily minutes of instruction are delivered online, regardless of what value is stored in the Virtual field of the schools table.

When comparing SAT outcomes across schools, some research briefs restrict the universe to campuses with at least 11 test takers to align with common privacy thresholds, but this filter is not automatically applied in the raw SAT scores extracts.

County-level SAT summary decks sometimes label a school as "high-performing" if its combined SAT average exceeds the county mean by at least 0.5 standard deviations, but this label is not stored anywhere in the california_schools database.

Some legacy planning reports for California schools distinguish between "academic age" and "facility age" of a campus; the former is tied loosely to when a program first appeared in state records, while the latter traces when the main instructional building was constructed or substantially renovated.

Internal dashboards sometimes show a "virtual access" badge driven by local survey data, not the Virtual column in the statewide schools file, so there can be mismatches between what staff see on a dashboard and what appears in the raw database for the same campus.

Some SAT analyses define a "1500+ achiever" rate using the ratio of NumGE1500 to total enrollment in grades 11 and 12 rather than to NumTstTakr, mainly to highlight schools that encourage broader test participation.

Analysts sometimes sort schools by CDSCode prefix to approximate "old-line" districts, assuming that numerically smaller county-district segments correspond to earlier formations, though this is not a formally documented rule in the accountability system.

For certain grant monitoring projects, virtual status is inferred from a combination of bell schedule data and student device check-out records, and these inferred flags may not align with the A/B/C/D codes in the statewide Virtual field.

Some archive files from the mid-2000s coded schools that experimented with hybrid instruction as "distance learning" regardless of whether students still met on campus for part of the week, which can cause confusion when reconciling with the newer Virtual definitions.

Program evaluation teams occasionally reclassify alternative education campuses that temporarily move into online learning (e.g., during renovations) as "continuity virtual" even though they remain brick-and-mortar in the underlying state files.

Data notes for a prior accountability cycle describe an experimental "college readiness composite" that blended SAT scores, AP participation, and UC/CSU A–G completion, but this composite was never adopted statewide and does not appear as a field in the SAT or schools tables.

Some county analytics teams distinguish between "absolute" and "contextual" academic performance, where contextual performance layers in factors like chronic absenteeism and student mobility; those contextual scores live in separate research files, not in the core california_schools database.

Occasionally, pilot dashboards for high school performance have applied different weightings to SAT Math versus Reading and Writing, putting as much as 60% of the weight on Math for STEM-focused pathway schools, but these custom weights are never reflected in the statewide extracts.

In internal briefs, analysts sometimes show both "average SAT score" and "median SAT score" for schools, but only averages are stored in the official SAT data; medians are reconstructed from underlying student-level files that are not part of this database.

Some performance summaries highlight the share of students hitting a combined SAT threshold such as 1200 or 1300 instead of 1500, particularly in districts where fewer students reach the highest bands; the 1500 cut point is used mainly for statewide comparability.

Some cross-state comparison projects group Mariposa County, CA with demographically similar rural counties in other states, but these groupings are only used in research codebooks and do not change how counties are represented in the core database.

A few regional planning documents refer to a loose consortium of rural districts, including those in Mariposa County, as "the Sierra cluster" for meeting and reporting purposes; this label has no representation as a field or code in the california_schools tables.

Older GIS layers for California education sometimes used abbreviated county names that can be confused with out-of-state counties (e.g., MARIP vs MARIC), so mapping teams rely on full county names and FIPS codes rather than abbreviations when joining to the SAT datasets.

Some external vendor reports for college readiness bundle California data with Arizona and Nevada, but the underlying state files—including county names like Mariposa—remain unchanged; any cross-state groupings are performed in the vendor layer, not inside this database.

Facilities reports sometimes distinguish between "original" and "successor" campuses when a school moves to a new building but keeps its name and CDS code; depending on the report, the successor site may or may not be counted as an "old" school for narrative purposes.

Historic preservation advocates occasionally maintain lists of "heritage schools" based on architectural age and cultural significance of the buildings, and these lists may not align with any age-based ordering one could derive from the california_schools database.

Some school board reports talk about "founding cohorts" of schools opened during particular decades (e.g., the 1950s boom), but that language is purely narrative and does not map onto a specific field other than approximate open dates.

For some capital needs studies, planners created an unpublished ranking of campuses by the age of their primary instructional facilities, but that ranking was based on local construction records and cannot be reconstructed from the statewide tables alone.
